name: Silicon Carbide (SiC) Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Silicon Carbide (SiC). Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for SiC surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal SiC absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective contaminant removal from SiC surface

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers used in ceramic cleaning; starting at 300 W balances efficiency and safety for SiC's high thermal conductivity (~120 W/m·K) to prevent overheating. Max avoids substrate ablation. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and study in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on SiC surface preparation.

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup; 2000 mm/s recommended for light oils/greases on SiC, slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy rust. Higher speeds for large areas. Source: Industry standards from Laserax cleaning systems manuals and Journal of Cleaner Production (2020) case study on ceramics.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of SiC; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) due to SiC's high melting point (~2700°C) and reflectivity (~40% at 1064 nm). Picosecond (ps) for ultra-precision but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulsed laser ablation of SiC.

repetition_rate.description: Standard for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve uniform energy delivery; 50 kHz recommended for SiC to balance speed and limit heat accumulation (trade-off: higher rates increase throughput but risk thermal stress). Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on repetition rate effects in ceramic laser processing.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe contaminant ablation; SiC damage threshold ~6-10 J/cm² at 1064 nm, so 2 J/cm² recommended for removal of oxides/particles without substrate etching. Conservative for SiC's robustness. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021) and SPIE Proceedings on laser cleaning of semiconductors.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for focused cleaning; 200 μm recommended for SiC surface preparation to ensure precision on fine features while covering larger areas efficiently. Smaller for detail work, larger for broad decontamination. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning heads.

pass_count.description: Typically 1-3 passes for complete removal of light contaminants on SiC; multi-pass (2 recommended) for layered/heavy buildup to avoid single-pass overload and damage. Single pass viable for thin films. Source: Industry practice in Laser Cleaning Handbook (2022) and case studies on ceramic substrates in Materials Science and Engineering: A (2016).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; 60% recommended for SiC to optimize energy distribution and minimize uneven heating given its thermal properties. Higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies in International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (2019) on scan strategies for laser cleaning.
